summary: Gruber reflects on Apple's discontinuation of the iPod Touch, the last remaining iPod model. He notes the bittersweet irony that most people will simultaneously feel nostalgic and surprised the product was still being sold. The piece traces the iPod Touch's history from its 2007 debut as essentially an iPhone without the phone, emphasizing how its thinner, sleeker design consistently previewed the iPhone's future form factor. Gruber shares two personal anecdotes illustrating how the iPod Touch's industrial design was so forward-looking it could be mistaken for an unreleased iPhone prototype. He concludes that the iPod Touch was more than a budget iPhone alternative — it was a glimpse of where Apple's flagship was heading.
key_points: The iPod Touch was discontinued after its last update in 2019, still running an A10 chip from 2016 and never receiving Touch ID or Face ID
The original iPod Touch debuted just 10 weeks after the first iPhone, offering nearly identical specs without the phone or carrier contract
The iPod Touch's thinner form factor consistently previewed future iPhone designs — the original was 8mm thick versus the iPhone's 11.6mm
Apple's Newsroom site has quietly become a quality archive, a marked improvement over the ephemeral 'Hot News' page of the Jobs era
The iPod Touch served as both an affordable entry point to iOS and a design preview of Apple's hardware ambitions
money_quotes: I suspect most people reading this news will experience two thoughts, simultaneously: That's a shame, I loved the iPod. Wait, they were still selling the iPod Touch?
Not only did the iPod Touch never make it to the Face ID era of iOS device design, it never even gained Touch ID. It is, today, a bit of a retro device.
Turns out, 15 years ago, making an iPhone without the phone meant you could make something remarkably thinner.
It was like a vision of the iPhone's future.

My first thought was that he must be an Apple employee using an as-yet-unreleased iPhone prototype, inexplicable though that would be to do anywhere, let alone in a lobby filled with Apple-centric media.
More than just an iPhone without the phone, it was a preview of the iPhone's future. Much like, in some sense, the original iPod was, too.
The iPod Shuffle with buttons. Not the one without any buttons, which was a fascinating experiment in minimalism but clearly a mistake in hindsight.
key_insight: The iPod Touch's greatest legacy wasn't as a budget iPhone alternative but as a consistently forward-looking preview of Apple's industrial design ambitions for the iPhone itself.
